course_description:
The course aims to equip students with technical expertise and practical skills required across the open pit mine lifecycle from data collection and analysis, slope design and assessment, and monitoring, to post-failure remediation and mine closure. Key topics covered in this course include: (i) geotechnical model development, (ii) slope design and optimisation, (iii) slope monitoring and instrumentation, (iv) slope stability management - dewatering, rockfall, and large instability, (v) geotechnical risk management, and (vi) final pit closure - geotechnical perspective.

The course consists of seven online modules, each focusing on key aspects of the slope design and management, including foundational knowledge of geotechnical model construction, slope design and monitoring, geotechnical risk management, and slope closure. The seven modules covered are:

The Slope Design and Optimisation module will be further supported by the use of the Rocscience software package. Details and access instructions will be provided at the start of the course. Students are also encouraged to make use of any slope design software tools available at their respective sites, if they wish.

course_contacts.name: Associate Professor Zhongwei Chen
course_contacts.notes: Consultation available on Thursdays, 9:00–10:00 via Teams meeting or by emailing A/Prof Zhongwei Chen at zhongwei.chen@uq.edu.au.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
The course is offered in a hybrid format, consisting of the following components:
Self-paced online modules featuring written content, videos, interactive activities, and/or quizzes.
Regular online drop-in sessions to provide ongoing support throughout the learning journey.
An initial one-day, in-person workshop to give a brief overview of the course and facilitate open discussions and idea sharing.
A final one-day presentation session to review and reflect on the learning outcomes achieved.
course_aims: The course aims to equip students with technical expertise and practical skills needed for rock slope engineering. Students will learn to evaluate and apply integrated solutions for slope stability design and optimisation, groundwater management, rockfall mitigation, slope monitoring, and the closure of open pits and waste dumps, using industry tools and frameworks to support safe geotechnical practices across the open pit mine lifecycles.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically evaluate geological, structural, and geotechnical data collection techniques and interpret results to inform rock mass characterisation.
Develop and apply advanced geotechnical models and software tools to inform slope stability assessments and design decisions.
Design and optimize open pit slope and waste dump geometries by integrating geotechnical parameters, stability criteria, and economic constraints.
Assess and interpret data from advanced slope monitoring systems and instrumentation to detect instability indicators and inform proactive geotechnical decision-making.
Evaluate the impact of groundwater and surface water on slope stability and design effective dewatering systems.
Analyse mechanisms of rockfalls and structure dominant slope instabilities, apply predictive and mitigation techniques, and formulate remediation strategies.
Critically review existing geotechnical risk assessment frameworks used by Australian mining companies and develop risk management plans to address slope instability through case studies.
Design geotechnically stable open pit and water dump closure plans that meet regulatory standards.

assessment_summary.title: Final report presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: 10/11/2025 9:00 am; The presentation session will run from 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM. Individual time slots for each student will be announced once enrolment is finalised.

assessment_details.title: Project Brief
assessment_details.task_description: You will be required to submit a brief using the uploaded template (maximum three pages, including everything). The project brief provides basic information about your slope design and management project and is expected to include the following information: Introduction: Provide a brief description of the operation you have selected, as well as the basic geological and mining settings Problem statement: Provide context on a selected task (e.g., is it a new slope or an improvement to an existing slope). Consultation with your colleagues and supervisors is expected Project Objectives: Outline the main objectives of this project Project scope and methodology: Outline the scope (or tasks) of the design project and briefly describe the approach required to achieve the key objectives. Also, describe the required resources (e.g., data collection and software requirements) to conduct this project Project timeline: propose the timeline for each of the project objectives Project risks: Briefly identify the risks to successfully completing this project within the required timeframe and any mitigation strategies that can be put in place Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission will be via turn-it-in on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Feedback will be provided to students after 14 days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Progress update - slide pack
assessment_details.task_description: This component involves the reporting of progress via a brief slide pack (PowerPoint or PDF presentation) of no more than 20 slides covering the following elements: Background description Progress vs plan Results to date Remaining challenges and mitigation plan Conclusions and completion plan In this update, you will highlight the milestones reached so far and identify any challenges or hurdles encountered and strategies to address them. This assessment provides an opportunity to provide feedback and guidance prior to the completion and submission of the final report. Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. assessment_details.title: Final report presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 10/11/2025 9:00 am The presentation session will run from 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM. Individual time slots for each student will be announced once enrolment is finalised.
assessment_details.task_description: For this component, you will be required to prepare a 12-minute online presentation followed by 5-minute questions on the scheduled presentation day. This final presentation will provide an opportunity to share experiences and generate discussion between the course participants and industry representatives. This presentation should include the following: Introduction with a brief description of the project Description of methodology Results and analysis Reflection on mine closure from a geotechnical perspective Conclusions and recommendations Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions are limited to 14 days to ensure students can receive feedback with sufficient time to incorporate into their final submission. Students who are unable to present at the scheduled date and time should apply for an extension, with evidence that supports this. As this is a timed-assessment, Student Access Plans (SAPs) are not suitable supporting documentation.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Presentation sessions are scheduled during the workshop with multiple markers.

assessment_details.title: Final report
assessment_details.task_description: A final report of no more than 20 pages (excluding coversheet, table of contents/figures and appendices) must be submitted and should include but is not limited to: A one-page executive summary Background of the selected operation Description of your project scope and tasks Description of methodology Results and analysis Slope monitoring strategies Key geotechnical risks and management plans Mine closure considerations Final conclusions and recommendations Relevant references and appendices Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Revision week (15 Sep - 09 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Self paced online modules Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Kick-off workshop The one-day workshop is scheduled for 16 September at the UQ St Lucia campus. It will include an introduction to the course (e.g. structure and assessments), presentations from industry representatives, interactive exercises, and lab tours. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Revision week (22 Sep - 09 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Online one hour drop-in Q&A sessions Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Online Presentation The one-day online seminar is scheduled for 15 November, starting at 9:00 AM. Each student will have 15 minutes to present their work, followed by 5 minutes of questions from the audience, including lecturers and industry representatives. A detailed schedule will be provided closer to the start of the course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
